```json
"follow-redirects": "^1.14.4"

As a result, when you try to install Axios, it will resolve to `follow-redirects@1.14.7` by default.

body:
You can use resolutions in your package.json

```json
"resolutions": {
"axios/follow-redirects": "^1.14.4"
}
```

Then you can upgrade it with yarn with `yarn upgrade-interactive --latest`

body:
Have never like this kind of solution that makes the result sometimes be a object pair or an array. 
it's a disaster waiting to happen. someone on a backend expects an array and always do `body.baz.forEach(...)` then someone could handcrafted payload that only include one `baz` then it would break b/c it's now an object instead. it's just bad practices. 

i would rather do:
- `[...formData]` to get a `[key, value][]` or:
- `Object.fromEntries(fd)` to always get out a object.

body:
> @mbargiel  The nice thing with agents is that you don't have to worry about problems with follow-redirects: the tunnel is established in a lower abstraction layer, and once Axios has the socket, it's established properly to the end server. Redirects work over the tunnel.

This is actually how we resolved the proxy issue. With custom http/https agent, https-over-http proxy or https-over-https proxy is properly handled by the agent and is independent of proxy handling by axios. If time permits, I will share some write-up explaining our method.

Hi @jasonsaayman 
Thanks for the response. You may want to try this out with `strictNullChecks` or `strict` set to `true` in `tsconfig` in your IDE and you'll surely get the type error I mentioned in the PR description. Type definition currently clearly specifies that these can either be a function or undefined(because them being optional). 
You can also refer to https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#strictNullChecks 
According to the docs
>When `strictNullChecks` is `false`, `null` and `undefined` are effectively ignored by the language. This can lead to unexpected errors at runtime.
 When `strictNullChecks` is `true`, `null` and `undefined` have their own distinct types and you’ll get a type error if you try to use them where a concrete value is expected.
 
 If you still have any concerns, please reach out, I'd be quick to respond 👍

body: `./lib/` contains private Axios modules, they are not meant to be exported directly. They may be changed at any time, and such changes will not be considered a violation of backward compatibility. It is impossible to predict what undocumented features and how users will use them if `./lib/` gets officially listed as exported modules. This step will greatly complicate the support, refactoring, and further development of the project. Desired functionality can be requested to be added for official export via Axios module export if you really need it.

body:
@baraeb92 ah okay! in case you want a temporary workaround, [this may help](https://github.com/sgammon/axios/blob/2aeff49a636c4bf977de1d7b235c1ea2c14feb9f/examples/fetch/workers/axios-worker.mjs#L34-L43):

```js
const response = await axios.get(url, {
    adapter: 'fetch',
    timeout: 1000,
    responseType: 'text',
    headers: {
        'Accept': 'application/javascript, text/javascript, application/json, */*',
        'Accept-Encoding': 'identity',
        'Origin': url.origin,
    }
});
```

if you pass `Accept-Encoding: identity`, you are asking for an un-compressed version, so this **shuts compression off temporarily** for the request. it depends where you're using compression; if we're still talking about the Edge runtime, you may not actually _want_ to compress an upstream request/response, and only compress when returning to the user. for example:

```
|---------------|
| some service  |
|---------------|
   ^
   | fetched with axios
   | `Content-Encoding: identity` (no compression)
   |
|---------------|
| worker        |
|---------------|
   ^
   | compressed to user
   | `Content-Encoding: gzip`
   |
```

in upstream request scenarios like this, it can often be desirable to drop the extra compression step, especially if the two services are very close together.

compression is only unsupported when _requesting_ with the new adapter; it won't prevent you from using response compression on Workers or other runtimes like Next.js Edge.

on the other hand, if you're doing manual stuff with `arraybuffer` or `blob` or `stream`, it will have to wait 😅 hope this helps.

body:
i would not recommend using `cross-fetch` it have a dependency on v2.x while the newer version 3.x is ESM, only 

fetch could be imported lazily with dynamic `import('node-fetch')` or something even better would be to instead use undici or the built in fetch provided by NodeJS v18+ that dose not require any dependencies.
undici would require 1 dependency and NodeJS v16.7+ 

but NodeJS v16 is also currently the LTS version. so i think it would be good to let the user pick and choose their own implementation instead of having axios bring in the fetch impl they want or need. some folks might prefer to use fewer dependencies. 
let the developer be able to hand the FormData, Headers, Response, Request and co to axios more manually instead.

body:
@KaKi87 Well, not everything that is being worked on is done publicly, at least that which requires intensive research work, when the code changes too intensively to commit it remotely with a reasonable commit history and the code is scattered across dozens of branches to test approaches, hacks, and usability.
Behind the scenes, research, prototyping, and development of the new Axios core have been ongoing for 2 years as we squeezed almost everything we could out of the current implementation. Without a carefully thought-out system of plugins and hooks, Axios simply gets dirty and grows in size if we try to add some new features, requested by the users. The goal is to make Axios extensible, with a loosely coupled code base, where adapters represent only a minimal layer of logic, and the underlying logic is moved to the core and its plugins to avoid code duplication.
Since technically the fetch adapter had been implemented for a long time locally, but it was expected that it would be released when v2 was ready, the number of issues related to fetch was growing, it was decided to port it partly to the old codebase to release it as a separate PR, at least to "fix" Axios v1. Due to a lack of time and effort, the development and release of V2 were significantly delayed, so I see no reason for users to wait any longer for the fetch adapter.
That PR does not significantly affect the codebase outside of the adapter context and does not introduce breaking changes.
Although a great job has been done in this PR, however, this PR changes the code base too intensively, far beyond the context of the fetch adapter, and adds a cross-fetch dependency, which makes little sense for Node when Axios itself can switch from `http` to `fetch`, while at the same time not implementing all the basic xhr functionality yet. All this does not allow us to release it in 1.x. 
Unlike rewriting the core, adding an adapter should not significantly affect the rest of the codebase at least at initial PR.
Because this PR undergoes deep refactoring along with the implementation of the adapter, it will be almost impossible to merge all the other PRs in development due to the huge number of conflicts; in fact, you will have to rewrite the code when merging. It's always better to create small PRs that have a single goal and keep potential conflicts to a manageable level.

@sgammon Thanks for your efforts. Without a doubt, this is a great job. 🤘 I won’t say that it won’t be possible to integrate this in principle, but it definitely can’t be published in Axios v1. Perhaps we can return to this in the next major version, for now, we are only talking about version v1. The future major roadmap is not yet clear.

body:
@jasonsaayman The way this is implemented seems to make the project very difficult to maintain. Now we have completely separate types and type tests - this doesn't guarantee consistency and forces us to duplicate definitions and test code. Support for types definitions in such conditions will be too exhausting. 
Here we need to find a way to reuse definitions for both entry points as much as possible. In addition, the commonjs module is just a fallback.
Personally, I think there is no point in adding such complexities to an almost obsolete thing after a decade of project existence. It's not a big deal that users will see a named export in IDE for a CJS module that doesn't actually exist because they've always used the default export to work with it and this can be covered by Readme. 
But if we really need it, we should have generic types for the CJS entry point and another one for ESM that just re-exports the types as a named export like it was done in the JS code, but not totally independent files.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
I agree this is cumbersome. Unfortunately this problem comes with dual packaging.

Because this package defines `"type": "module"`, TypeScript considers `.ts` files to be ESM. `.cts` files can’t import ESM TypeScript files.

I think this could be resolved by removing `"type": "module"` from `package.json`. Then instead of a `.cts` and `.ts` file for CommonJS, we get a `.ts` and `.mts` file. The `.mts` file can import from the `.ts` file and some logic could be reused.

Note that simply removing the CJS type definitions and keeping `"type": "module"` tells TypeScript this package can’t be imported in CommonJS at all. For example:

1. clone https://github.com/remcohaszing/axios-test-instance (Doesn’t include these updated types at the moment of writing)
2. Run `npm install`
3. In [`index.ts`](https://github.com/remcohaszing/axios-test-instance/blob/main/index.ts), remove the `@ts-expect-error` comment from the axios import.
4. Observe the error.

The simplest solution would be to just remove CJS support altogether, but that would be a massive breaking change.

@remcohaszing It seems that axios@1.2.0-alpha.1 can already be used in the TS project with import and require, this is covered by tests:

- https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/test/module/ts-require-default/index.ts
- https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/test/module/ts-require/index.ts

Currently, both entry points have a factory export that has consistent typings for TS, but only ESM module has named export as an alternative.
The problem is not so significant - TS users will see the named export in autocompletion even for the commonJS module, where it is not present. That's the entire problem, so all this PR is about autocompletion, not importing Axios in TS context.
I think this is acceptable, especially considering that trying to fix this inconvenience would result in a significant overengineering of the project design.

> I think this could be resolved by removing "type": "module" from package.json.

The package is an ESM JS module that requires "type": "module" in order for the import statements to work natively in JS. CJS is just a fallback for `require`.

> The simplest solution would be to just remove CJS support altogether, but that would be a massive breaking change.

Removing the entire cjs module just because TS users will have some inaccuracies in their types, this is definitely not a solution.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
> @remcohaszing It seems that axios@1.2.0-alpha.1 can already be used in the TS project with import and require, 

Yes, that’s because this pull request is part of that release.

> this is covered by tests:
>
> * https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/test/module/ts-require-default/index.ts

This doesn’t test any types. It uses:

```ts
const axios = require('axios').default;
```

This means `require` is used as a function, not as an import. This function is typed as a function returning `any`, so axios is `any` in that file. The correct way to require modules in TypeScript is

```ts
import axios = require('axios')
```

So all this test does, is the same as https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/test/module/cjs/index.js at runtime.

I suggest to remove this.

> * https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/test/module/ts-require/index.ts

This is the same situation as above, except it tests the `axios.default` property that was added recently works at runtime.

> * https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/test/module/ts/index.ts

This does test the axios types, but very minimally. It asserts the types work with these very specific compiler options:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "module": "commonjs",
    "moduleResolution": "node",
    "esModuleInterop": true
  }
}
```

The [`"node"`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/module-resolution.html#node) module resolution predates `package.json` exports. It uses the old `types` field in `package.json`. Because ESM and CJS aren’t properly interoperable, TypeScript 4.7 and greater added the [`node16` module resolution](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/esm-node.html).

The types **are** tested using `dtslint` and these files:

- `/test/typescript/axios.cts`
- `/test/typescript/axios.ts`
- `/tsconfig.json`
- `/tslint.json`

> Currently, both entry points have a factory export that has consistent typings for TS, but only ESM module has named export as an alternative.

I’m not sure what you’re trying to say here. ESM has a default export and a number of named exports. CJS had `module.exports`.

> The problem is not so significant - TS users will see the named export in autocompletion even for the commonJS module, where it is not present. That's the entire problem, so all this PR is about autocompletion, not importing Axios in TS context. I think this is acceptable, especially considering that trying to fix this inconvenience would result in a significant overengineering of the project design.

There seems to be a misunderstanding here! This pull request has nothing to do with autocompletion. This is about correctness of the TypeScript types, and the ability to use axios in type checked CJS. Although this might lead to better autocompletion in editors that rely on TypeScript (such as VSCode).

> > I think this could be resolved by removing "type": "module" from package.json.
> 
> The package is an ESM JS module that requires "type": "module" in order for the import statements to work natively in JS. CJS is just a fallback for `require`.

The package could still support dual packaging. `"type"` only changes how `.js` and `.ts` files are interpreted. The difference is that what is now published as `.js`, should be published as `.mjs`, `.ts` → `.mts`, and `.cts` → `.ts`.

I’m not saying this should be done, but if it was, the `.mts` file could import and reuse types from the `.ts` file to prevent duplication. If you do find a way to deduplicate them, I would love to hear it!

> > The simplest solution would be to just remove CJS support altogether, but that would be a massive breaking change.
> 
> Removing the entire cjs module just because TS users will have some inaccuracies in their types, this is definitely not a solution.

I would seriously consider this for a next major version, but it’s not my call to make. Anyway, thanks to TypeScript 4.7 and this PR, it won’t be necessary for the TypeScript part.

---

TLDR: CJS and ESM are significantly different. Different workarounds in the past have led to more compatibility issues. Modules look easy, but they are hard! There are many aspects to it a lot of people don’t know.

TypeScript makes it even more complex. Before TypeScript `4.7` the best thing we had was `"moduleResolution": "node"`. This implementation assumes CJS is used. TypeScript 4.7 added `"moduleResolution": "node16"`. This adds support for dual publishing using the `exports` field in `package.json`, although it is a bit cumbersome for package authors.

body:
> Yes, that’s because this pull request is part of that release.

This PR was merged later, so tests don't rely on it, in addition, index.d.cts is currently incompatible with index.d.ts / actual export.

> This doesn’t test any types.

And they shouldn't. These tests ensure that the Axios entry points are consistent and the module can be imported into the target environments, so the users can use it. 

> The package could still support dual packaging.

It already supports this for JS and TS environments, the only problem is **`any` type instead of "AxiosStatic" when loaded with a "require" statement in TS**. That's all.

But we can:
- use Axios without explicit typings when loading it with `require`
- use `import` statement instead of `require` in TS and have explicit typings without having separate typing for each entry-point

So the question here is, is it worth adding type support for imports with the `require` of increasing design and maintenance complexity?

>The difference is that what is now published as .js, should be published as .mjs, .ts → .mts, and .cts → .ts.

Yes, but this looks like a hack, and it would be nice to have normal js files for a plain JS project.

> I would seriously consider this for a next major version, but it’s not my call to make. 

Not everyone uses TypeScript and the current version of Axios works fine in both ESM & CommonJS styles in JS as well as TS with `require` & `import` statements.

> > Yes, that’s because this pull request is part of that release.
> 
> This PR was merged later, so tests don't rely on it,

I thought you meant you tried this in [`axios-test-instance`](https://github.com/remcohaszing/axios-test-instance). Indeed, the tests don’t rely on it, because as I explained, the CJS tests don’t use the TypeScript types at all.

> in addition, index.d.cts is currently incompatible with index.d.ts / actual export.

How do you mean it’s incompatible?

> > This doesn’t test any types.
> 
> And they shouldn't. These tests ensure that the Axios entry points are consistent and the module can be imported into the target environments, so the users can use it.

Then I suggest to remove the build step from these tests. There are only four runtime entry points:

- ESM Node.js
- CJS Node.js
- ESM browser
- CJS browser

> > The package could still support dual packaging.
> 
> It already supports this for JS and TS environments, the only problem is **`any` type instead of "AxiosStatic" when loaded with a "require" statement in TS**. That's all.
> 
> But we can:
> 
> * use Axios without explicit typings when loading it with `require`

This statement is false! As explained above, the referenced tests don’t use `require` correctly. The correct way to use require in TypeScript is:

```ts
import axios = require('axios')
```

Let’s put it differently. TypeScript can be used to type check JavaScript code.

```jsonc
// tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "checkJs": true,
    "moduleResulution": "node16"
  }
}
```

```js
// This is a type error
const axios = require('axios')
```

> So the question here is, is it worth adding type support for imports with the `require` of increasing design and maintenance complexity?

The question you should ask is: Should Axios support type definitions? If yes, this PR fixed CJS support in TypeScript. If no, then it’s better to remove all type definitions from the repository and move them to [DefinitelyTyped](https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped) instead.

---

You seem to be downplaying the severity of this issue based on a lack of knowledge on how TypeScript modules work, especially on how this has changed since TypeScript 4.7. This more complex than your comments, please don’t underestimate it. This makes it hard to have a discussion about this subject.

I suggest you read the following resources:

- https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/modules.html (old module system)
- https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/esm-node.html (new module system)
- https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-4-7/#esm-nodejs (actually seems to do a better job at explaining it than the handbook)

I also suggest you really fiddle with the [`axios-test-instance`](https://github.com/remcohaszing/axios-test-instance) source code, as it’s a simple real world example that shows the issue.

---

I do acknowledge it’s not ideal to duplicate type definitions. I have asked if type definitions can be reused between CJS and ESM in the TypeScript Discord help channel and am awaiting a reply.

author: jasonsaayman

body:
Hey @jasonsaayman ! We have to realize here what percentage of users will be affected by the lack of typings for the commonjs module, the possible workarounds for users, and the reasonableness of solving this problem.
Since, in my opinion, this affects only a small percentage of TS users, so I do not understand the importance of solving typings lack of a fallback entry point if there is no simple/cheap solution.

As I see it, there are the following cases in TSConfig:

- module: `CommonJS` + `require` statement => axios `common js` entry point is used => types missing (any)
- module: `CommonJS` + `import` statement => axios `common js` entry point is used => we have proper typings (AxiosStatic)
- module: `ESNext` + `import` statement => axios `ESM` entry point is used => we have proper typings (AxiosStatic)

The common js entry point is mostly there for JS users to use without babel, TS users can just use an import statement and have the correct typings with conversion to CommonJS or ESM. Or I'm missing something? Is there a reason to use `require` instead of `import` in actual versions of TS and projects that use it?
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
We currently live in a situation where the JavaScript ecosystem is slowly transitioning from a CJS to ESM. This means package authors need to make a choice: publish CJS, publish ESM, or publish both CJS and ESM.

Prior to Axios 1, only CJS was supported. Yes, there was a `"module"` entry point that’s understood by module bundlers, but as far as types are concerned, only `"main"` mattered.

The correct way to use Axios back then, was:

```js
const axios = require('axios')
```

The correct type definitions for this were:

```ts
declare namespace axios {
  // …
}

declare function axios(/* … */): // /…

export = axios
```

However, many people do not understand how to properly type CJS modules. Instead, the following types were published:

```ts
declare function axios(/* … */): // /…

export default axios
```

According to these types, this was correct usage:

```js
const axios = require('axios').default
```

So to accommodate this, the runtime `default` property was added.

This is not just the case for Axios. Many packages did this wrong, and many still have wrong type definitions today.

---

Various tools added different ways to attempt interoperability between ESM and CJS. Often people would write ESM syntax, then compile to this:

```js
export default function foo() {}
```

into this:

```js
function foo() {}

module.exports.default = foo
```

TypeScript added the `esModuleInterop` flag to support this when compiling to CJS. This would add some boilerplate code that attempts to resolve compiled default exports. So this:

```ts
import foo from 'foo'
```

Becomes this:

```js
"use strict";
var __importDefault = (this && this.__importDefault) || function (mod) {
    return (mod && mod.__esModule) ? mod : { "default": mod };
};
Object.defineProperty(exports, "__esModule", { value: true });
const foo_1 = __importDefault(require("foo"));
```

This option is fine when building an end-user library, such as a website or server, but not desirable for libraries.

---

Node.js 12 added native support for ESM. To support compatibility with CJS, the `module.exports` field would be interpreted as the default export. So in order to consume the prior code in ESM, one would have to type this:

```js
import foo_exports from 'foo'

const foo = foo_exports.default
```

This is not great, but that’s how many libraries are developed. Now the problem is, TypeScript didn’t understand how to consume this. So TypeScript would show errors for that code.

Not only did Node.js 12 add support for ESM, but also for the `"exports"` field in `package.json`. This field allows dual publishing CJS and ESM.

---

Prior to TypeScript 4.7, TypeScript did not support dual publishing. They added an new `"moduleResolution": "node16"` option to accommodate this. As a shortcut, it’s also possible to use `"module": "node16"`, which sets a couple more options. Also the `.cts` and `.mts` extensions were introduced to represent `.cjs` and `.mjs` files. This is a huge step forward for ESM and dual publishing support for TypeScript and the npm ecosystem as a whole.

This new module resolution looks at the `package.json` fields `"type"` and `"exports"`. Just like the `"type"` field is used how `.js` files are interpreted by Node.js, it’s also used to determine how `.ts` are interpreted by TypeScript.

If a module contains `"type": "module"` in `package.json`, this tells TypeScript that `.d.ts` files are a module. This means the package can’t be consumed using CJS. However, packages can dual publish using the `"exports"` field. Since there are runtime entry points for ESM and CJS, there also need to be TypeScript entry points for ESM and CJS.

Now I believe that if a package claims to support CJS, and it claims to support TypeScript, it should also support using it with the combination of CJS **and** TypeScript. That’s what this PR did, it added a CJS entry point for TypeScript.

| Module           | ESM       | CJS                      |
| ---------------- | --------- | ------------------------ |
| runtime code     | Supported | Supported                |
| type definitions | Supported | Support added in this PR |

---

Now I agree 100% with @DigitalBrainJS that supporting type definitions for both CJS and ESM is tedious. Unfortunately this responsibility comes with dual support. If someone could point me in a direction how types could be reused, I would love to hear about it.

Older versions of TypeScript don’t know about `.cts` files. So the `.ts` file can’t import from the `.cts` file.

CJS can’t import ESM, so the `.cts` file can’t import from the `.ts` file.

One solution is to remove `"type": "module"`, so `.js` and `.ts` files are treated as CJS, and publish `.mjs` files. Personally I don’t love it, but it could allow some reusability of the types.

> would move to DefinitelyTyped help bring down the overhead of maintaining both files? Also would typing the entire Axios project and going to a full TS approach remove the need for managing two sets of definitions?

No. This would move the burden and the discussion to the DefinitelyTyped repository. Since the goal of DefinitelyTyped is to provide correct type definitions, they would accept the types as I have provided them.

This would also be a breaking change which users won’t like. A package removing TypeScript support feels like a step backwards for most users.

Just like removing TypeScript support would remove the need to maintain duplicate type definitions, so would removing support for CJS or ESM. I’m not saying you should, but I am saying it’s an option if the type definitions are too much of a burden.

---

I see some value in the tests in https://github.com/axios/axios/tree/v1.x/test/module. I understand it’s valuable to test the CJS and ESM entry points. I don’t really see the point of the tests that are compiles from TypeScript first. As @DigitalBrainJS pointed out, these do **not** test the types. In fact, the `ts-require-default` and `ts-require` tests don’t even use the Axios types at all. They use `const axios = require('axios')`, which is not how `require()` should be used in TypeScript.

> > This doesn’t test any types.
>
> And they shouldn't. These tests ensure that the Axios entry points are consistent and the module can be imported into the target environments, so the users can use it.

This means they are just variations to test the CJS runtime entry point. (`package.json` and `tsconfig.json` matter). They might as well be compiled with Babel or ESBuild.

It is important to note TypeScript is **not** just a build tool. It is also a type checker for both TypeScript code **and** JavaScript code. To provide TypeScript support, means to provide type definitions.

---

Now for a practical example, let’s create a new project. This example deliberately doesn’t use `.js` or `.ts` files, because whether they are ESM or CJS depends on the `"type"` property in `package.json`.

```jsonc
// package.json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "axios": "1.1.3",
    "typescript": "^4.9.3"
  }
}
```

```jsonc
// tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    // Tell TypeScript to also validate JavaScript files.
    "checkJs": true,
    "module": "node16"
  }
}
```

```ts
// mts.mts
// Uses the ESM types
import axios_mjs from 'axios'

// Uses the CJS types
// Compiles to:
// import { createRequire as _createRequire } from "module"
// const __require = _createRequire(import.meta.url)
// const axios_cjs = __require("axios")
import axios_cjs = require('axios')

// Uses the ESM types
const axios_mjs_dynamic = await import('axios')

axios_mjs('/')
axios_cjs('/')
axios_mjs_dynamic.default('/')
```


```ts
// cts.cts
// Uses the CJS types
// Compiles to:
// const axios_cjs = require("axios")
import axios_cjs = require('axios')

// Uses the ESM types
import('axios').then((axios_mjs) => {
  axios_mjs.default('/')
})

axios_cjs('/')
```


```js
// mjs.mjs
// Uses the ESM types
import axios_mjs from 'axios'

// Uses the ESM types
const axios_mjs_dynamic = await import('axios')
```


```js
// cjs.cjs
const axios_cjs = require('axios')

// Uses the ESM types
import('axios').then((axios_mjs) => {})
```

Now run `npm install` to install TypeScript and Axios.

From the project root, run `tsc --noEmit true` to check types. Note how this yields errors for all CJS use cases.

```sh
$ npx tsc --noEmit true
cjs.cjs:1:27 - error TS1479: The current file is a CommonJS module whose imports will produce 'require' calls; however, the referenced file is an ECMAScript module and cannot be imported with 'require'. Consider writing a dynamic 'import("axios")' call instead.

1 const axios_cjs = require('axios')
                            ~~~~~~~

cts.cts:2:28 - error TS1471: Module 'axios' cannot be imported using this construct. The specifier only resolves to an ES module, which cannot be imported with 'require'. Use an ECMAScript import instead.

2 import axios_cjs = require('axios')
                             ~~~~~~~

cts.cts:9:1 - error TS2349: This expression is not callable.
  Type 'typeof import("node_modules/axios/index", { assert: { "resolution-mode": "import" } })' has no call signatures.

9 axios_cjs('/')
  ~~~~~~~~~

mts.mts:5:28 - error TS1471: Module 'axios' cannot be imported using this construct. The specifier only resolves to an ES module, which cannot be imported with 'require'. Use an ECMAScript import instead.

5 import axios_cjs = require('axios')
                             ~~~~~~~

mts.mts:11:1 - error TS2349: This expression is not callable.
  Type 'typeof import("axios/index", { assert: { "resolution-mode": "import" } })' has no call signatures.

11 axios_cjs('/')
   ~~~~~~~~~


Found 5 errors in 3 files.

Errors  Files
     1  cjs.cjs:1
     2  cts.cts:2
     2  mts.mts:5
```

Now update Axios, so the types from this PR are included.

```sh
npm install axios@1.2.0-alpha.1
```

Now run the type checker again to notice the type erros are gone

```sh
$ npx tsc --noEmit true
```

---

> We have to realize here what percentage of users will be affected by the lack of typings for the commonjs module, the possible workarounds for users, and the reasonableness of solving this problem.

I would like to point to this chart, taken from [wooorm/npm-esm-vs-cjs](https://github.com/wooorm/npm-esm-vs-cjs).

![State of ESM vs CJS](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wooorm/npm-esm-vs-cjs/main/index.svg)

According to this chart, 77.4% of the most popular packages in the npm ecosystem use CJS (including dual). Now I think Axios has quite a lot users who create websites or backends. Those projects probably use ESM or faux ESM. These are not represented here. Still, a lot of people use CJS.

Also TypeScript 4.7 is relatively new. `"module": "node16"` is the correct setting for Node.js projects, but many people are not aware of this yet or are blocked by their dependencies not supporting this yet. Most people don’t fully understand modules in the first place and blindly accept any advice to make TypeScript shut up, without understanding what it actually means.

For example: _“Just enable `"esModuleInterop"`”_, _“Just use `const foo = require('foo')`”_, or _“Just add a `@ts-ignore` comment”_.

I believe Axios, being a popular package, can really help the ecosystem move forward by doing it right.

body: Yes, Axios exports its factory only as a default export, and all other exported properties defined in the factory are also available as named export. It seems like this behavior has a logical fit with the existing commonjs interface. Basically, it's not a big deal to re-export the factory as a named export too, but historically Axios had only default export, so all the tutorials are written for default export. I may be wrong, but having `axios` and `Axios` as named export might confuse some users.

body: @jasonsaayman IMHO hotfixes are best released separately from minor changes, as such releases have a higher priority. This is more reliable because we can fix one thing but break another, probably not covered by tests, and the whole release becomes useless. Therefore, if we have a broken minor release (with a critical regression bug), we should release a patch to fix it, and only then publish a minor release with other changes.

body:
@DigitalBrainJS thanks for hoping on this so quick.  I've spent some time looking over this issue and it looks like it stems back from trying to support `AxiosHeaders` class as a valid type of `header` property on the `AxiosRequestConfig` type.  I wonder if there's really any value to supporting this use case?  

https://github.com/axios/axios/commit/7a2f901f42138e1ef89c63ee9ac89a0d7a630077#diff-243f86a2e64131743ca04681df5d21438e79fcabe06f18029cb8dcef6a70121fR351

I've seen other request libraries express headers as an Object/Map so is there really value to supporting a custom class like `AxiosHeaders` in the config?  In my case, as a library that wants to allow consumers to pass an axios config but add my custom headers (i.e. CONTENT-TYPE / AUTHORIZATION), the library will need to detect which type of header collection you are passing (Record or AxiosHeaders) and update accordingly.  

If you think you need to support both, I'd suggest making the `AxiosHeaders` constructor accept the other `AxiosRequestConfig['headers']` type of `RawAxiosRequestHeaders`.  Right now it only takes `type RawAxiosHeaders = Record<string, AxiosHeaderValue>` which doesn't sufficiently overlap `type RawAxiosRequestHeaders = Partial<RawAxiosHeaders & MethodsHeaders & CommonHeaders>;`

That way, if a client passes a config of either `headers` type, the `AxiosHeaders` class can handle converting it keeping both `header` types somewhat interoperable.
author: jonesmac

body: This might need your attention @jasonsaayman
author: baraeb92

body: @jonesmac Yes, the need to support `AxiosHeaders` as config headers was due to the previous bug #5226 - some plugins (`axios-retry`) and users re-use config inside interceptors by passing internal config directly to a new Axios request. This is more of a backward compatibility fix than a new feature, although users can now use AxiosHeaders as a helper for case-insensitive header preprocessing (merge, delete).

body:
This will probably break at least CDN compatibility, as they currently use the default entry point to deliver the UMD build in a pure script tag. Even if some of them respect the browser field and the require subpath, it refers to the cjs module, not the UMD.
Currently, it can't be just replaced. If such an edit were made, then the script tag would have to explicitly specify `type="module"` so that it could load the esm module, otherwise, it would expect UMD. 
So, this change has serious consequences that could affect a huge number of users, and that's definitely a breaking change, in any case, not allowed in a non-major release.

body:
> But the problem is that package.js directs us to use axios/index.js, for both the browser and Node.

It simply means that you misconfigured your build tool and it builds a node.js version of your package dependencies tree.  
https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/2e70cecda42993e1153248f0f96715c3c55f7f39/rollup.config.js#L33

> Somehow you've removed TextEncoder completely from dist/browser/axios.cjs.

Well there's no magic here- that is just a well-known conditional export/import and their old prototype - [`browser` field in package.json](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/2e70cecda42993e1153248f0f96715c3c55f7f39/package.json#L128-L132)

author: wkeese

body:
> I'll just point out that Axios allowed consumers to have a single (multi-platform) build until version 1.2.1.

I really hope our consumers don't do that although this could explain why there are some people on the Internet who think that Axios weighs 2 MB :)
This was never possible unless you packaged all the native node modules with something like browserify in a huge bundle for the client, which is absurd since only a few percent of the code will be client-side related. But if we consider this acceptable, then nothing has changed since version 1.2.1 - just another import of the native node module has been added, exactly the same as all the others - http, zlib, stream, etc. This was never the intended ability, so it's not an Axios issue whether the bundler has an actual shim for the native node module import or not.

body:
Assuming semantic commits [https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/#:~:text=BREAKING%20CHANGE%3A%20a%20commit%20that%20has%20a%20footer%20BREAKING%20CHANGE%3A%2C%20or%20appends%20a%20!%20after%20the%20type/scope%2C%20introduces%20a%20breaking%20API%20change%20(correlating%20with%20MAJOR%20in%20Semantic%20Versioning).%20A%20BREAKING%20CHANGE%20can%20be%20part%20of%20commits%20of%20any%20type.), you need to either trail your commit with `BREAKING CHANGE`, or add an `!` to the commit type.

eg:
```
fix!: change order to push in pushRequestInterceptors
```

or:
```
fix: change order to push in pushRequestInterceptors

BREAKING CHANGE: Reverses the order of the request interceptor execution
```

body:
> the project was switched to "type": "module" and "./index.js" became an esm file instead of commonjs, however, the "main" entry in package.json still points to "index.js". 

Why do you think this should not be so? Axios is ESM module and according to the documentation, the main field must point to an ESM module if the package has "type: module". "Module" field is not in npm spec and it is just a proprietary field for some bundlers. According to the npm documentation, backward compatibility with commonjs for an ESM package should be handled by conditional export.
Axios should follow the official specification, and not adapt to special cases arising from bugs, obsolescence, and inconsistencies of third-party libraries. If a third-party library has a bug or lack of support for functionality, then it must be resolved on its part. 
In addition, node v12.0-12.7 does not support conditional export so the main field will be used.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
> Why do you think this should not be so? Axios is ESM module and according to the documentation, the main field must point to an ESM module if the package has "type: module".

I haven't found that in the node documentation specifically that implies the main field must be the es-module the package type is module. I could only find documentation that suggested that it could point to cjs or esm (in which case the package would not be consumable from a `require()` expression because you can't require esm files.

For example, there is the following quote:
> All currently supported versions of Node.js and modern build tools support the "exports" field. For projects using an older version of Node.js or a related build tool, compatibility can be achieved by including the "main" field alongside "exports" pointing to the same module:
> ```
> {
>  "main": "./index.js",
>  "exports": "./index.js"
> } 
> ```

While that says the can point to the same module, their example doesn't use conditional exports or specify `"type": "module"`, so I think the documentation is unclear. That certainly would be compatible with old versions of node assuming index.js is commonjs (which it would be given the extension and lack of type entry), but if that was an es-module it would fail to be `require` in old versions of node, and you would only support `import` in node 12.0-7. Since you can both `import` and `require` cjs entries from cjs and esm, it follows that setting the main entrypoint to point to the commonjs entry would be the most compatible way to specify the main entry for cases where exports is not used.

https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html
https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html#modules-ecmascript-modules

> Axios should follow the official specification, and not adapt to special cases arising from bugs, obsolescence, and inconsistencies of third-party libraries [...] In addition, node v12.0-12.7 does not support conditional export so the main field will be used.

So I tested this in node 12.0 and as you say, the conditional export isn't used and so the following code fails to work in older versions of node where the main field is used instead of the export map:

```js
const {default: axios} = require('axios');
axios.get('http://www.google.com').then(result => console.log(result.data));
```
Fails with:
```
import axios from './lib/axios.js';
       ^^^^^
SyntaxError: Unexpected identifier
```

But node versions 12.0-7 were never LTS versions, and they are certainly out of date, so maybe we don't need the main field at all if your intention is to not support special cases as a result of obsolescence.

I guess my question is, if we are relying on the exports map for this functionality to work correctly in node, what is the intended use-case for setting the "main" entrypoint to point to the esm version of the package? The field will never be used in current versions of node and breaks in old versions, so maybe it is because that is what would be used by bundlers that don't support the exports map? If that's the case, those same bundlers support the proprietary "module" and "browsers" entry-points.

There's also a ton of other entries already in this `package.json` for supporting use-cases outside of the npm specificaton:
https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/7a60e50ab97860a908cee831f99d00a1e3b4ef0c/package.json#L140-L146C32

I can maybe see the case for using:
```json
"browser": {
  ".": "./index.js",
```
instead of `"module": "index.js"` assuming the two would be equivalent if you didn't wan't to add another non-standard field, but I still think setting the main field to point to commonjs is within specifications and improves compatibility.

@DigitalBrainJS  The Mock Service Worker (msw) library uses jsdom or happy-dom and does not run in a browser. It is for Unit Testing and Integration Testing. We have one codebase with axios@1.5.0 and another identical codebase with axios@1.5.1. The tests work fine and all pass when using MSW with 1.5.0, but the change to 1.5.1 now breaks any calls that mock an API call. It also looks like someone logged an issue on the MSW repo that and also here in this repo.

author: bignamehere

body:
@bignamehere Yes, that is an MSW issue with mocking XMLHttpRequest inside Axios' xhr adapter.
Axios 1.5.0 forced the use of `http` adapter in Node.js, which caused problems for users in testing, so this change was reverted in version 1.5.1.
Thus by default, Axios `1.5.0` uses `http` adapter for Node.js, and `all other versions` use the first available adapter from the list `['xhr', 'http']`. In the case of emulating a browser environment in Node.js, this will be `xhr`. However, due to the bug in MSW, an error occurs when using it.
So you can switch the adapter back to `http` (but you must understand that it will not be possible to use functionality specific to the xhr adapter in tests) or wait for MSW to fix it.
```js
axios.defaults.adapter = 'http';
```

author: Fallsleep

body:
Hey. MSW creator here. Let me clarify a few statements around this thread.

> The Mock Service Worker (msw) library uses jsdom or happy-dom and does not run in a browser. It is for Unit Testing and Integration Testing. 

MSW has nothing to do with JSDOM/happy-dom. It's _your_ choice what environment you decide to use MSW in. MSW also runs in both the browser and Node.js. 

> Yes, that is an MSW issue with mocking XMLHttpRequest inside Axios' xhr adapter.

This statement is a bit misleading. Here's the right hierarchy of request modules and how MSW integrates into that:

- You (developer) use `axios`
  - You (developer) configure `axios` to use the `xhr` adapter.
    - Axios uses the `XMLHttpRequest` constructor to issue requests. That constructor doesn't exist in Node.js, so this implies you (developer) are using some sort of browser-like environment for Node.js (e.g. JSDOM/happy-dom).
      - JSDOM/happy-dom construct a new `XMLHttpRequest` and proceed with executing it.
      - MSW proxies the `XMLHttpRequest` constructor to intercept requests and mock their responses.

There's no MSW/Axios adapter relationship at all. MSW operates on a much lower level than both Axios and its adapter (and even JSDOM). 

Thanks for pointing out this error only happens in Happy DOM! This is an important distinction. I will take a look what Happy DOM does with XHR `timeout` and see where the issue truly lies. On MSW side, `ontimeout` property on XHR instances has a setter that allows MSW to correctly respect the custom set timeout.

author: scott-knight

body:
@scott-knight 

- As I said earlier, the issue is due to an incorrect build process, and not due to Axios. These constants have been defined [since node v0.5.8](https://nodejs.org/api/zlib.html#constants). This code is not imported into browser builds at all so it can't lead to errors. If this bug was in Axios itself, then no one would be able to use it after version 1.2.1, at least in the node environment. Node v12...20 and the browser platforms are covered with tests. This should lead you to believe that the problem is local.
- To describe 'everything', you need to follow the issue template. There is neither a target platform nor an example that demonstrates the bug. We don't have enough resources to check every bug report without exhaustive information, even if it were truly an Axios issue.
- Axios package has node and browser [bundles that are built with rollup](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/dist/axios.js). So this is definitely a local ESBuild issue and most likely due to incorrect user configuration. The Axios team is not able to fix anything outside the project.
- ESbuild [builds Axios normally on both platforms](https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/axios-esbuild-lyl794?file=%2Fpackage.json%3A13%2C22)

So I don't know what else can be added here. If you are sure that the issue is really caused by Axios, please feel free to provide a reproducible example and we'll do our best to fix this.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
@DigitalBrainJS Thanks for the reply. I appreciate the points you've made. However, I'm gonna disagree.

- ESBUILD configurations are pretty straightforward and simple. There isn't anything to configure in ESBUILD to get buggy, newer versions of axios to compile correctly. As mentioned, the axios code obviously changed (which is causing this issue) after version 1.2.0. To ignore that fact is silly. You yourself said the CONSTANTS have been included since Node version 0.5.x. Compiling of axios brakes in our project after version 1.2.0. We didn't change anything in our code that would cause this, obviously, the axios code changed. To lay the issue of that responsibility at the feet of our project is incorrect. I posted what I could in the bug report template. I apologize if what I posted wasn't enough (which I believe I pointed out), however, it appears that at least one dev ([tusharv01](https://github.com/tusharv01)) was able to post a reasonable fix with with the information provided (the only change I would make is optional chaining within the call  `exports?.constants?.Z_SYNC_FLUSH || 2`, to avoid failure in the chain call).
- Since we're not able to use updated versions of axios, and there doesn't appear to be fix forthcoming (based on your comments), we're actively changing out axios code in favor of vanilla JS `fetch`.   

I realize I'm poking the bear by being blunt, but there it is. Please feel free to close this PR and close my bug post. I wish you and the axios team the best.

This is a breaking change. Should force a major version bump. Here's why:
* I send CSRF token as a cookie, with samesite lax, for domain ".domain.com"
* Just as an example, there's a "frontend.domain.com" and a "backend.domain.com".
* The header is no longer being sent to backend.domain.com, but the cookie is (as it matches .domain.com)
Projects might update to this version and suddenly API calls start to fail, and the reason isn't obvious.
The solution I have is to set it in our interceptor now.

body:
Honestly, I'd prefer having an implementation of a domain whitelist parameter.

This list would contain trusted domains to which the XSRF token is consistently sent. Domains not present in this whitelist would automatically be excluded from receiving the XSRF token. 

By adopting this approach, we not only standardize our protocol but also minimize potential human errors. With the current solution, we would have to configure the `withXSRFToken` parameter every time we want to handle it differently than the initialized value.

Thoughts?
author: xHeaven

body:
@xHeaven I think that this should not be the flag replacement, otherwise, it forces the developer to duplicate hosts in the url and ACL in the case of simple requests, without inherited configuration. Of course, you can add it to the global configuration, but this is an extra action too. If the project already uses the `withCredentials` option, then the Axios instance tree is probably built so as not to duplicate the configuration for it. IMHO adding an `withXSRFToken` option next to withCredentials would be the simplest and most concise way to fix the current situation. 
I am inclined to believe that the issue of ACL implementation should be left to a separate PR. In essence, its functionality can be achieved through the current inheritance of configurations so we need to consider the question of whether it is worth complicating the Axios, how much demand this function can have, and what exactly the ACL should control - only withXSRFToken, withCredentials or mixing the entire config for this path/domain.

author: jasonsaayman

body:
@jasonsaayman Are there any updates? 
IMHO, we should fix the current situation in the `1.x` branch by providing an equivalent solution ASAP (although several weeks have already passed, but still), otherwise, we are now forcing users to write their own implementation of reading cookies and setting XSRF tokens inside interceptors. I suspect they're not too thrilled about this.
I believe the only reason this can be considered a vulnerability is that the `withCredentials` option in `XMLHTTPRequest` does not pass any tokens, which may be a surprise to a developer less familiar with Axios specifics, because no one reads the documentation when everything seems to be working fine.
Therefore, the minimum possible solution is to make setting the token a separate option, then the developer will be forced to set this option, next to `withCredentials` explicitly. Such behavior would in no way be considered a vulnerability.
The ACL implementation should be higher-level functionality and should not be the only way to send a token. Besides, the design of the API and its implementation takes a relatively long time (as probably want wildcard support or something like that), which delays the publication of the fix.
A future ACL implementation may take into account when `withXSRFToken` === `undefined`, the same can be done for `withCredentials`, so the explicitly set value will take precedence over the ACL.
> the CVE (even though I do not think it is one) 

Judging by the fact that we both do not consider this a real vulnerability, it was probably worth challenging the CVE report :)

author: jasonsaayman

body:
The vulnerability here ([CVE-2023-45857](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-wf5p-g6vw-rhxx)) states that the problem is that Axios will include the XSRF token in HTTP headers to _**any**_ host

Example use cases are given here too: https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/6006

I think the problem will be if the developer is using Axios to make API calls to their own (trusted) domain and also to 3rd-party domains (i.e. to consume a web service).  Using the new `withXSRFToken` will simply re-introduce the vulnerability since it still isn't checking what host the HTTP request is being sent to. 

The developer could use the `withXSRFToken` setting safely by only setting it for API domains they trust but I suspect this is likely to be overlooked by developers hoping to 'plug-and-play' Axios into their code. 

I have no idea how easy/hard this would be to implement but this would be my ideal:

- When `withXSRFToken` is not set, XSRF token is not included in HTTP headers
- When `withXSRFToken` is set Axios includes the XSRF token in HTTP headers when the target of the HTTP request is the same domain that issued the XSRF token in the first place (Axios would have to keep track of that somehow).
- `trustedDomains` option - overrides the above by including the XSRF token in all whitelisted domains the developer specifies.

PS: Appreciate the effort being made here regardless. ;) Sorting this sort of thing out is often harder than it sounds...

author: xHeaven

body:
@adarrel753 

> Using the new withXSRFToken will simply re-introduce the vulnerability since it still isn't checking what host the HTTP request is being sent to.

IMHO developers are not children, to protect them from potentially dangerous actions. Our task is to ensure that Axios options do not mislead developers. If the developer explecitly sets the `withXSRFToken` option in a request, then he knows exactly what he is doing for that target. The `withCredentials` option could be misleading, but `withXSRFToken` clearly states its functionality, so it won't be a surprise forthat the token will be sent. This is the only thing that could potentially be considered a CVE.
Since Axios allows you to create instances, nothing prevents you from creating an instance with `withXSRFToken` installed and making requests from it to trusted origins, and/or inheriting other instances.
Axios has given the developer the ability to control the sending of the token, so he can control the setting of withXSRFToken inside the interceptor, or provide a function as the value for withXSRFToken and now the developer can implement the ACL functionality himself.
Although the implementation of the ACL filter has already been implemented in #6077, the feasibility so far looks questionable, especially in the context of the fact that this is client code and every extra byte is undesirable.

Is there a reason for the [workflow to be running `npm install`](https://github.com/axios/axios/actions/runs/7198852489/job/19880139621?pr=6091#step:4:1) rather than `npm ci` for the Node `12.x` and `14.x` checks when y'all are committing the `package-lock.json` file? I was only able to reproduce the error  in CI locally after regenerating my lock file. All of the dependencies in y'all's `package.json` file are careted and, notably, [deterministic builds as a default were not introduced until NPM 7](https://github.blog/2021-02-02-npm-7-is-now-generally-available/#changes-to-the-lockfile) (i.e. Node 15+). I believe that's causing the discrepancy and, consequentially, the error within the `node_modules` directory.

**TL;DR**: Can I change the install command to `npm ci` for the `12.x` and `14.x` checks or are y'all intentionally avoiding deterministic builds on them?

body: Is there any reason we can't simplify this solution to `/\/?\/$/` or `/\/{1,n}$/`? The second regexp `/^\/+/` is not vulnerable to ReDoS as it has linear complexity, so there is no reason to filter its input.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
> Is there any reason we can't simplify this solution to `/\/?\/$/` or `/\/{1,n}$/`? The second regexp `/^\/+/` is not vulnerable to ReDoS as it has linear complexity, so there is no reason to filter its input.

However, please note that using `/\/?\/$/` or `/\/{1,n}$/` does not handle cases where multiple slashes need to be replaced, as demonstrated by the example: `'///foo' + '/'.repeat(100000) + 'bar////////'`.
In the second case, if you are certain about it, I can remove the filter.
author: WillianAgostini

body:
> does not handle cases where multiple slashes need to be replaced

I don't see any reason why we need to normalize multiple trailing slashes since it's an invalid url. We just need to join the base url and relative path and there is no point in complicating the code for an impractical case. So for now I'm I tend to the simplest implementation.

> In the second case, if you are certain about it, I can remove the filter.

Well, this is quite obvious, since the graph of the regexp has no branches, only a direct loop. This can be confirmed with a test service like https://devina.io/redos-checker or your test case.

body:
Thanks for putting this together, @SerhiyZheliznjak, and apologies for the long silence on our side.

We're going to close this one rather than merge. A few reasons:
- Breaking change on v1.x. Removing the R generic and reordering parameters silently miscompiles existing call sites — `axios.post<MyResp, AxiosResponse<MyResp>, MyData>(...)` is a documented, widely-used form, and after this change the type arguments would land in the wrong slots with no error. That makes it semver-major, not a fix.
- Inconsistent generic ordering between methods. As @clouds56 noted, request/get/delete/head end up `<T, D>` while options/post/put/patch/\*Form end up `<D, T>`. Same T letter, opposite position per method, no error, and no way to fix without a breaking change. Again, that makes it semver-major.
- Scope is incomplete. index.d.cts has the same signatures and isn't touched, and the Axios callable interface (AxiosInstance) overloads further down index.d.ts aren't updated either, so the default `axios.post(...)` would diverge from new `Axios().post(...)`. There are also no type tests in test/module/typings/ exercising the new shape.

The underlying UX win (drop the middle slot) is genuinely worth doing, it just needs to land as a coordinated breaking change with one consistent ordering applied to index.d.ts, index.d.cts,
and AxiosInstance together, plus type tests.

Closing for now but happy to revisit on the next major. Thanks again for the proposal and for kicking off the discussion in #6236. 🙏

```JavaScript
        promise = promise

            .then(result => {prevResult = result ? result : prevResult}) // update the result
            .catch(chain[i++]) // push the onRejected handler
            .then(() => prevResult); // pass the result to the next interceptor
```

**Orb Code Review** (powered by GLM-4.7 on [Orb Cloud](https://orbcloud.dev))

## Summary

This PR adds a new `progressUpdateIntervalMs` configuration option to control the frequency of progress events in Axios. The implementation updates the fetch, http, and xhr adapters to respect this new setting, along with type definitions, tests, and documentation.

## Architecture

The approach is generally sound: calculate a `ticksRate` (events per second) from the `progressUpdateIntervalMs` (milliseconds between events) and pass it to the existing `throttle` helper via `progressEventReducer`. The `progressEventReducer` function accepts a `freq` parameter that controls the throttling frequency.

## Critical Issues

### 1. **Critical Bug in xhr.js - Wrong Parameter Order** ⚠️

In `lib/adapters/xhr.js`, the upload progress event handler has a critical bug:

```javascript
// Current (WRONG):
request.upload.addEventListener('progress', progressEventReducer(_config.onUploadProgress, progressUpdateTicksRate));
```

The `progressEventReducer` signature is:
```javascript
export const progressEventReducer = (listener, isDownloadStream, freq = 3) => {
```

The code is passing:
- 1st arg: `onUploadProgress` ✓ (correct)
- 2nd arg: `progressUpdateTicksRate` ✗ (WRONG! Should be `false` for `isDownloadStream`)
- 3rd arg: Missing! (Should be `progressUpdateTicksRate`)

**Impact**: Upload progress events will be treated as download progress events (`isDownloadStream` becomes truthy), and the throttling frequency is not being set correctly. This breaks upload progress tracking.

**Fix**:
```javascript
request.upload.addEventListener('progress', progressEventReducer(_config.onUploadProgress, false, progressUpdateTicksRate));
```

### 2. **Missing Flush Handler in xhr.js** ⚠️

The original code in xhr.js used the returned `flush` function from `progressEventReducer` to ensure the final progress event fires on `loadend`:

```javascript
// Original code:
([uploadThrottled, flushUpload] = progressEventReducer(onUploadProgress));
request.upload.addEventListener('progress', uploadThrottled);
request.upload.addEventListener('loadend', flushUpload); // This is now missing!
```

The PR removed the `flush` handler, which means the final progress update when upload completes might be missed if it's throttled.

### 3. **Missing Input Validation**

The code checks if `progressUpdateIntervalMs` is a number but doesn't validate that it's positive:

```javascript
if (utils.isNumber(config.progressUpdateIntervalMs)) {
  progressUpdateTicksRate = 1000 / config.progressUpdateIntervalMs;
}
```

If `progressUpdateIntervalMs` is 0 or negative, `progressUpdateTicksRate` would be `Infinity` or negative, which could cause unexpected behavior.

**Recommendation**: Add validation to ensure `progressUpdateIntervalMs > 0`.

## Minor Issues

### 4. **Commented-Out Test Code**

In `test/specs/progress.spec.js`, the upload progress assertions are commented out:

```javascript
expect(downloadProgressSpy).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);
// expect(uploadProgressSpy).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);
```

These should either be uncommented (with proper test setup) or removed/commented with an explanation of why they're not tested.

### 5. **Inconsistency with fetch.js Pattern**

In `fetch.js`, the code passes the `isDownloadStream` parameter explicitly:
```javascript
progressEventReducer(onUploadProgress, false, progressUpdateTicksRate)
```

But in `xhr.js`, the same pattern is not followed (see Critical Issue #1).

## Cross-File Impact

The changes affect:
- All three adapters (fetch, http, xhr) - progress event handling
- Type definitions (`index.d.ts`, `index.d.cts`) - adds new config option
- Tests (`test/specs/progress.spec.js`) - adds throttling tests
- Documentation (`README.md`) - documents new feature

Due to the bug in xhr.js, users of the XHR adapter (browsers) will experience incorrect upload progress behavior if they use the new `progressUpdateIntervalMs` option.

## Assessment

**Status**: ❌ **Request Changes**

**Reasoning**: The PR has a critical bug in the xhr.js adapter that would cause incorrect behavior for upload progress events. The parameter order is wrong, and the missing flush handler could cause the final progress update to be skipped. Additionally, input validation is missing.

**Recommendations**:
1. Fix the parameter order in xhr.js for upload progress
2. Restore the flush handler on upload `loadend`
3. Add validation for positive `progressUpdateIntervalMs`
4. Uncomment or explain the commented-out test assertions
5. Consider adding a test case for invalid `progressUpdateIntervalMs` values

The feature itself is valuable and well-designed conceptually, but the implementation needs these fixes before it can be safely merged.

**Orb Code Review** (powered by GLM 5.1 on [Orb Cloud](https://orbcloud.dev))

## Summary

Excellent TypeScript fix resolving "cannot be named" export errors when using `RawAxiosHeaders` in generic contexts. Changes interface to type literal to fix module export visibility.

## Issue Analysis

### **Root Cause**
TypeScript "cannot be named" error when exporting generics that reference local interfaces:
```typescript
// ❌ Problematic: local interface causes export issues
interface RawAxiosHeaders {
  [key: string]: AxiosHeaderValue;
}

// ✅ Fixed: type literal is properly exportable
type RawAxiosHeaders = {
  [key: string]: AxiosHeaderValue;
};
```

### **Technical Background**
TypeScript treats `interface` and `type` differently for module exports:
- **Interface**: Creates a named declaration that may not be exportable in all contexts
- **Type literal**: Creates an anonymous object type that's always exportable

## Code Quality ✅

### **Fix Strategy**
```typescript
// The change resolves this error:
export const RequestHeadersContext = createContext<RawAxiosHeaders | undefined>(undefined);
// Error: "cannot be named" → Fixed ✅
```

### **Implementation Details**
- **Semantic equivalence**: Both interface and type literal have identical runtime behavior
- **Export compatibility**: Type literal resolves TypeScript module boundary issues
- **TSLint comment**: Acknowledges intentional deviation from interface-first preference
- **Consistent application**: Applied to both `.d.ts` and `.d.cts` files

## TypeScript Best Practices

### **Interface vs Type Choice**
**When to use `interface`:**
- Extensible contracts
- Object-oriented patterns
- Declaration merging needs

**When to use `type`:**
- Export constraints (like this case)
- Union types, mapped types
- Complex type transformations

### ✅ **Correct Application**
This is a textbook case where `type` is the right choice due to export requirements.

## Cross-file Impact

**Minimal risk:**
- **Backwards compatible**: No breaking changes to public API
- **Type equivalence**: Consumers see identical type structure
- **Export fix**: Resolves user compilation issues without side effects

## Developer Experience Impact

### **Positive Outcomes**
- **Compilation fixes**: Resolves "cannot be named" errors for users
- **Generic usage**: Enables proper use in React contexts and other generic scenarios
- **Type safety**: Maintains all existing type checking benefits
- **Library adoption**: Removes barrier to using Axios types in complex applications

## Assessment: ✅ **APPROVE**

This is a well-executed TypeScript compatibility fix:

1. **Addresses real pain point**: Fixes actual compilation errors users encounter
2. **Technically correct**: Uses the appropriate TypeScript construct for the use case
3. **Zero risk**: Type-equivalent change with no runtime impact
4. **Proper execution**: Applied consistently across both TypeScript definition files

**Recommendation**: Merge immediately. This resolves a legitimate TypeScript compilation issue without any downsides.

**Technical note**: The reason `type` works here is that TypeScript handles type aliases differently in module exports, allowing them to be properly "named" in external contexts where interfaces might not be accessible.

body:
**Orb Code Review** (powered by GLM-4.7 on [Orb Cloud](https://orbcloud.dev))

## Summary

This PR adds `create` to the named exports in `index.js`. The `create` function exists in the axios default export but was missing from the named exports, causing issues when users try to import it via named import in certain bundling scenarios.

## Architecture

**Files Changed**:
1. `index.js` - Added `create` to imports and exports (+4 lines)
2. `test/specs/api.spec.js` - Added test for `create` function (+4 lines)

**Changes**: 8 insertions

## Assessment

**Status**: ✅ **Approve**

## Positive Aspects

### 1. **Fixes Real Issue** 🐛

The PR addresses a real problem:

> We are having an issue in our project, that for some reason undiscovered yet, `create` is not available through default export after bundle.

The `create` function exists in the default export but was missing from named exports.

### 2. **Simple and Focused** 🎯

The PR makes a minimal, targeted change:

```javascript
// Added to imports:
const {
  ...
  create
} = axios;

// Added to exports:
export {
  axios as default,
  create,  // NEW
  ...
};
```

### 3. **Adds Test** 🧪

The PR includes a test to verify the export:

```javascript
it('should have create properties', function () {
  expect(typeof axios.create).toEqual('function');
});
```

### 4. **Consistent with Other Exports** 📋

The change follows the pattern of other named exports like `Axios`, `AxiosError`, `getAdapter`, etc.

### 5. **Non-Breaking** ✅

Adding a named export doesn't break existing code that uses the default export or doesn't import `create`.

### 6. **Addresses Bundling Issue** 📦

The PR mentions a bundling issue where `create` wasn't available after bundling. This is likely because some bundlers handle named and default exports differently.

## Minor Suggestions

### 1. **Consider Adding More Tests**

The current test just checks that `create` exists. Consider adding a test that verifies it works:

```javascript
it('should have create function that creates axios instances', function () {
  const instance = axios.create({
    baseURL: 'https://api.example.com'
  });
  
  expect(instance).toBeDefined();
  expect(instance.defaults.baseURL).toBe('https://api.example.com');
});
```

### 2. **Check if create is Already Tested**

The PR author notes:

> Obs.: The test added do not fail in case of not being exported

This suggests the test might not actually verify the export correctly. Consider:

```javascript
// Test that named import works:
it('should allow named import of create', function () {
  // This would fail if create is not exported
  // However, this test needs to be in a separate file to test actual imports
  pass;  // Placeholder
});
```

### 3. **Consider Adding Documentation**

If this is a common issue, consider adding a note in the README or migration guide:

```markdown
### Named Imports

All axios utilities can be imported by name:

```javascript
import { axios, create, getAdapter, AxiosError } from 'axios';
```
```

### 4. **Verify the Root Cause**

The PR mentions:

> for some reason undiscovered yet, `create` is not available through default export after bundle.

**Investigation Opportunity**: It would be helpful to understand which bundler has this issue and why. This could help prevent similar issues in the future.

Common bundlers to test:
- Webpack
- Rollup
- esbuild
- Vite
- Parcel

### 5. **Check Other Functions**

Are there other functions that might have the same issue? Consider reviewing the default export to ensure all important functions are also exported by name.

### 6. **Consider Export Pattern**

Looking at the imports:

```javascript
const {
  ...
  create
} = axios;
```

This pattern destructures from the default export. Consider if there's a more direct way to import these functions from their source modules, which might be more reliable for bundlers.

However, the current pattern is consistent with the rest of the file, so this is fine.

## Cross-File Impact

This PR only affects the module exports. The change is:
- Non-breaking
- Backward compatible
- Enables a new import pattern

## Questions for Maintainers

1. **Bundler Investigation**: Which bundler(s) had the issue with `create` not being available? Understanding this could help prevent similar issues.

2. **Other Exports**: Should we audit the default export to ensure all important functions are also exported by name?

## Conclusion

This is a straightforward PR that fixes a real export issue. The implementation is minimal and follows existing patterns. While the test might not fully verify the fix (as noted by the author), the change itself is correct and necessary.

**Required Changes**: None

**Optional**:
1. Add a test that verifies `create` actually creates an instance
2. Investigate which bundler(s) had the issue
3. Consider auditing other exports for similar issues

Simple and correct fix! 🎉

body:
Thanks for sending this in, and sorry it sat for a while.

This is already handled on v1.x. lib/adapters/fetch.js it now runs every feature probe through a small test() wrapper that swallows throws and returns false, so an environment that rejects a ReadableStream body to `new Request(...)` just makes supportsRequestStream resolve to false instead of crashing the module.

body:
+1, works for me too.

But there seems to be another issue related to the Streams API using Fetch.

https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/c6cce43cd94489f655f4488c5a50ecaf781c94f2/lib/adapters/fetch.js#L158-L168

Calling the last line `let response = await fetch(request);` will throw an error: `net::ERR_H2_OR_QUIC_REQUIRED`, which means when request body is `ReadableStream`(returned by `trackStream()`), only HTTP2 or HTTP3 is supported.

This is troublesome for local development or environments that do not support HTTP2 or HTTP3.

Any ideas for a solution?

Related issue:
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/76412619/receiving-error-neterr-h2-or-quic-required-when-using-fetch-api-with-a-strea
